Music We like those who are like us, or who are similar to us. Research has shown how this applies even to a seemingly minor similarity, a similar-sounding name. The researchers mailed surveys to people after editing the name of the sender to be similar to, or different from, the name of the person receiving the survey. For example, in the similar names condition, if the recipient's name was Robert Greer, the sender's name was listed as Bob Greger, whereas in the dissimilar condition, the sender of the survey's name would be listed as Mark Moore. The same was true for all the female recipients in the study, so if the recipient's name was Cynthia Johnson, in the similar name condition, the sender of the survey was listed as Cindy Johansson, whereas in the dissimilar condition, it would be listed as Beth Green. You get the idea. Here's the evidence of how impactful this small similarity was in producing social influence. In all cases, the survey sender asked the recipient to complete and send back the survey within one week. When the researchers looked at the numbers of recipients who complied with the sender's request, they were shocked. If the names had been made to look similar, the recipients were twice as likely to comply with the sender's request. Why would something so trivial have such an impact on compliance? Because people like those who are like them, even in small ways, and people say yes to those they like. Now, if something as minor as a similarly sounding name can drive compliance with a request, think of how much more consequential larger commonalities can be, such as similar backgrounds or interests or attitudes. A key takeaway is that before we begin to do business with or make a request of someone we don't know well, we should take the time to learn about the individual's history, likes, dislikes, hobbies, and so on. And there are two main ways to do so. The first is simply to go online to learn what the individual tells us on social media. This isn't proprietary information, it's what folks want others to know about them. The other route is to arrange an opportunity for informal, sociable interaction, such as meeting for coffee, or asking about family in the minutes before a conference call, or getting together for lunch before an important business meeting. It doesn't have to be formal or elaborate. Even casual interactions can offer opportunities to register and remark on genuine similarities that build rapport before getting down to business.
